comments.title: Taking up Druidry
comments.content:
Hey, it's me; obnoxious journal guy from last week.

Background) I've been fairly consistent in my Druidry practice over the last week. The results have been both terrific... and terrifying. I'm both impressed and overwhelmed, and possibly a bit scared. Right out of the box, the SoP was able to induce a massive change in both myself (reaching equilibrium) and the feeling of the space. Almost overnight, a really strong bond with nature formed; and communication with both plants and animals comes really intuitively. When I've taken a day off or started to second guess my decision to practice Druidry, this fades almost immediately. Also, there have been multiple instances where I feel an enormous presence; twice it's identified itself as Hu and once as Elen. Both incidents with the former were clearly benevolent but also immensely powerful; the latter was really strange and somewhat confusing, like it was more interested in mental plane contact but didn't want to manifest any lower. I haven't had any connection to celtic spirituality in the past and my default approach to dealing with spirits is more rooted in east Asian culture. Dealing with them through anything other than prayer feels like a massive slide in the wrong direction and possibly disrespectful. Intuitively, I feel like this entire system is targeting my deepest weaknesses, and getting properly into this is going to require a great deal of pain and suffering.

1) Does Druidry dig up an abnormal amount of past "stuff" to deal with in comparison to other systems? Is it suitable for somebody with an unusual background / karmic debt?

2) I have extensive prior experience in eastern systems, but I haven't been practicing intensively for several years. Is this enough to preclude me from Druidry? Are there some other precautions I should be taking?

3) Are there any known interactions between Druidry and Hypnosis?

4) Is it correct that we don't choose our gods, but rather our gods choose us? How should I be dealing with them / this?

replies.title: Re: Taking up Druidry
replies.content:
1) Western occultism generally does a good job of helping you deal with your unfinished business; that's true of old-fashioned Druidry as much as any other Western occult system. As for whether it's suitable for you, well, you're going to have to deal with it sooner or later; facing it now, willingly, is going to be a lot easier on you than trying to run from it and having it clobber you later.

2) You should be fine. Just don't mix them now.

3) Not particularly. There are some Western occult systems that don't mix well with hypnosis, such as the Golden Dawn tradition, but the third Grand Archdruid of the Ancient Order of Druids in America was a hypnotherapist by profession.

4) Relationships with gods are just as varied and uniquely personal as relationships with human beings. In either case, relax and focus on clear communication, and you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: Taking up Druidry
replies.content:
I'm in a similar place to the OP, and wanted to share my own thoughts that can hopefully help the OP on their path.

I’m also just starting SOP, have been working on other aspects of my spirituality for a while such as discursive meditation, very light divination using tarot, and slowly working my way through the cosmic doctrine. For a long time I was content with remaining on the speculative side of things, but after meditating on a few events in my life, decided starting an SOP practice was the right thing to do.

I felt compelled to nod in violent agreement with JMG and your own experience about digging up old stuff. It's definitely going to happen, but as I've been progressing on my own path, I've realized just how talented I was at ignoring what was truly going on in my internal life. You can't run away from it, and hiding from it becomes more and more unsustainable as time goes on. It's tough, but no matter what you're facing, the best course of action is to be strong (or, if you're like me, pretend to be strong!) and face it.

My personal experience with the SOP has been a clarity around my own mental issues. I still "feel" really strongly, but I'm a bit more likely to be able to get to a place where I can "step outside" of that feeling. It's hard to explain: I'm still feeling it and it still hurts like hell, but I'm a bit more able to see it for what it really is and be less consumed by it.

replies.title: Re: Taking up Druidry
replies.content:
At the risk of sounding like a whiny kid,

I was never speculative. I jumped in with both feet as soon as I got the chance. There was a decade long period where I was intensely disciplined in my work. Having more than few people get in over there head though, I really admire the cautious, the tentative, and the theorists.

I actually went through the entire LRM process years ago, but it was nothing like this. Much has changed between now and then. Bringing up old stuff wasn't in any doubt. I was just wondering if it was more intense than other systems in that regard. Buddhism feels both familiar and more gentle, but it also feels like I might be ignoring a key part of this incarnation in the process.

It's a delight to hear about somebody both seeing and moving towards balance like that. There are other techniques that I can use to deal with pain, but they seem to involve me slipping rapidly into the Vamachara, and it really feels wrong to go back to that.

I really like Greer, and he is probably my favorite living occult author. I just have a really hard time accepting the idea that a choice of system doesn't really matter and that I can just pick one at random. In any case, thank you for sharing. If it wasn't helpful, it was at least endearing.

replies.title: Re: Taking up Druidry
replies.content: And I admire those with the confidence to jump in the way you have! I'm my own worst enemy in that regard.

comments.content:
In a recent Mundane Astrology lesson over at your Subscribestar/Patreon, you talked about Lunation Charts. You gave basic instructions for how to read lunation charts for the new moon, and mentioned full moon charts in a way that made it sound as if they have inconsistent results.

If so, I have a conjecture as to why this might be. A regular ingress chart tracks the progress of the Earth around the Sun over the course of a solar year, keeping track of ingresses that happen at the natural inflections of the solstices and equinoxes. A lunation chart does the same for the Moon around the Earth— but instead of solstices and equinoxes, these points would be called the four quarters of the moon: New Moon, First Quarter, Full Moon, and Last Quarter.

So, my hypothesis would be that the rules that apply for an ingress chart of a solar year also apply for any other astrological planet, scaled to their own “year”. Thus, in the case of a Lunation chart, if a Quarter is attained in the midst of a cardinal sign, then the chart would only apply for a single quarter. If a Quarter is attained in a mutable sign, then the chart would apply for two quarters, or until the next New Moon. And if the Quarter is attained in fixed sign, then its effects last all the way until the next New Moon. Some inconsistent results in a Lunation chart might thus be due to a failure to apply these rules.

Does this sound like a reasonable hypothesis to you? Is there something I’m misunderstanding or neglecting to take into account? (If this does seem plausible, yes, I do intend to try testing this once I manage to progress a little bit further my astrology studies.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's a reasonable hypothesis, but as far as I know, nobody's ever explored the possibilities of first and last quarter charts. It seems worth testing.

comments.title: Renovating like an Archdruid
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

An interesting theme turned its head this week.
My significant other and myself are a bit of DIY nuts. She is also the only one that knows what I am doing when I say that I am going “to go sit for half an hour” :-)
This week we were discussing woodworking projects, and she suggested making a meditation chair. Love her, but it was strange trying to explain to her, that an ordinary kitchen chair is perfectly suitable and has the added benefit of camouflage. For further work; the coffee table is also suitable along the same lines. And for the times that I go in the woods an old square peace of a board is just perfect.

But I also just read the four novels by Dion Fortune, where the characters dedicate whole rooms and houses as working spaces. If I may, as food for the imagination;
a.) How does the practice space of a decades long practitioner and an archdruid look like these days?
b.) Well we do not all have unlimited funds, but what would you set up, if you had the funds of Hugh Patson?

Best regards,
V

replies.title: Re: Renovating like an Archdruid
replies.content:
1) A cluttered spare bedroom that contains many bookshelves, a desk, a filing cabinet, and an electric organ, among other things. I've never had a space that I could set aside purely for magical practice; that space is also where I write my books and blog posts.

2) If I had the money? A plain, more or less cubical room with a skylight, not very large. Off-white walls. A shallow shelf/altar on the eastern wall, with a few ritual items in it. An inconspicuous closet to one side, containing robes and other hardware. I prefer a very spare magical aesthetic -- think Zen monasteries and Shaker churches.

I'd also have a lodge room somewhere for group workings, but that's another matter.

replies.title: Re: Renovating like an Archdruid
replies.content:
1. Thank you. I thought it would be funny to give you a question where I could picture you looking around the room and shrugging. Also it was a good demonstration of the practice, where I could point out to my partner, that yes it really is a budget friendly activity. She said “ it just takes time”. It is also something I can imagine most occultists going for as opposed to ...

2. ... the over the top settings of Dion Fortunes books. The properties and renovation projects she paints would be worthy of that British TV series Grand Designs. Somehow this seems to be a pattern within her fantasy books. Fast cars, unique real-estate, and some convenient way to finance it all. Your picture is lovingly different. Would the floor have to be on ground floor or could it work in an attic of a barn?

replies.title: Re: Renovating like an Archdruid

The habit of giving adepts lavish digs is a pervasive tic in fantasy fiction; Kathering Kurtz's series The Adept had the same repetitive habit, and it really gets dull. I've made a firm resolution that the majority of my fictional adepts shall live in ordinary houses or apartments.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good evening Archdruid.

1) A few weeks ago there was a discussion here on what happens to people who die prematurely, i.e. by suicide, freak accident, ect. Correct me if I'm wrong but I think you said that such people are stuck in this world after 'death' with all their bodies except the material body until the end of what would have been their natural lifespan. I also remember from a discussion much further back (which may not have involved something you said directly...could have been from some of the commenters) that when a person is left wandering around without their physical body, their etheric body still needs to 'feed' (on other etheric energy, presumably?) in order to sustain itself, otherwise the etheric body breaks down; the wandering soul must 'vampirize' the living in order to stay 'alive' (for lack of a better term). I'm confused by these conflicting accounts, or perhaps I'm just misremembering. Is the need to 'feed' postmortem just for souls who expired due to old age? And that those who pass on young are still energetically 'fed' until their natural old age? Finally, do young murder victims have to stick around for many years in etheric/astral form?

2) Sort of related to the above....Going through Ancient Egyptian funerary texts, it seems like the priests were obsessed with avoiding the Second Death and that this avoidance was also a popular religious belief. Do you have any insights into why the Ancient Egyptians were so fixated on this? Could this simply have resulted from a misunderstood remnant of much older practices that had a practical purpose for high-ranking individuals who needed to stick around after death for one reason or another?

3) Would an individual having a mere intellectual understanding of the occult mechanics of death, afterlife, and rebirth have a significant effect on their own death and afterlife experience?

-Magenta Malodorous Owl

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The need to "feed" is a function of an etheric body exhausted by old age or illness. A young and healthy etheric body doesn't need that for quite some time. As for murder victims, that can happen -- that's why murder victims sometimes become ghosts -- but since they didn't choose their state, they have an easier time getting out of it.

2) That's a very complex issue. Egyptian funeral practice was indeed focused on avoiding the Second Death; what happened was that a magical technology with other uses was hijacked to allow the rich and powerful to avoid the consequences of their karma, and then got institutionalized.

3) No, not by itself. Combine it with spiritual practices and it can do a lot of good.

Since Egyptian mummies are all at least several hundred years old even in the early 19th century, is it probably that the souls had already gone on to Second Death? replies.title: Re: Mummies
replies.content: By the time the first European archeologists got to them, the most recent mummies were much more than a thousand years old -- mummification stopped when Christianity seized power and outlawed traditional Pagan practices -- so yes, almost certainly the souls were long gone. Any that happened to remain would have dissipated as soon as the mummy was exposed to sunlight.

comments.title: Freedom
comments.content:
It is frustrating to deal with friends, family, and so many others who think that my conservative views are some kind of personal moral failure. Logic does not work against doublethink, and I end up feeling lonely and finding solace in online videos that get me worked up about how liberty is dying.

The advice I got from meditation was to ask myself if I want to be right or do I want to be free, and then take the next logical step towards the latter.

It is difficult though because it feels like I should stand up for what I believe in before it is too late, but I need to find a way that is not a waste of time pointlessly bringing up negative emotions and destroying relationships

Any suggestions?

replies.title: Re: Freedom
replies.content:
I'd encourage you to reflect on the idea that "standing up for what you believe in" amounts to getting into arguments with family and friends that you know will just result in a miserable experience for all concerned. You might consider instead finding a community, online or off, where you can discuss your political views with people who can handle them, and stop having political conversations with those who can't.

You may find, if you do this, that your friends and family start pushing back, trying to get you to defend conservative views to them so they can denounce you and feel morally superior. Don't give them that pleasure. Roll your eyes, say something like "Why bother? You just believe whatever the corporate media tells you," and walk away. Distance and silence are powerful tools. Learn to use them.

replies.title: Re: Freedom
replies.content: I think the best way to stand up for your values and goals in the political arena is in the ballot box, organizing with like-minded folks, working on a campaign, running for an office, etc.  Trying to convince the other side they are wrong is a lost cause.  Pick your battles, and choose the ones you can win.

replies.title: Re: Freedom
replies.content:
Good advice all around so far.

I would add: try to be creative about how you engage those around you. Part of what you’re feeling may be due to the expectations you have of the conversation. It may be an expectation of trying to change ones mind, or of simply having a thoughtful conversation without having the other person resort to groupthink.

I personally lean more liberal, but I’ve found no matter what the persuasion of my conversation partner, I can usually increase the odds of a positive conversation by having my intention be to understand why the other person thinks the way they do.

Do this for a bit and you’ll begin to notice a few things. You’ll notice when your friends of any political stripe are thinking for themselves, and when they’re resorting to groupthink. It will also help you discover more nuance in your own ideas.

replies.title: Re: Freedom
replies.content:
I like to ask questions. I saw a woman with two bumper stickers that were the same - Trump Lied, People Died. I asked her what they were referring to. She said the insurrection of 1/6. Oh, I said, who died? Well, that police officer, she replied.

How did he die?

He was beaten with a fire extinguisher, I think.

Hmmm... his autopsy report said he had no blunt force trauma.

Well, then he was sprayed with bear spray.

Oh, that must be it. Except the chief medical examiner said there was no evidence that he had a reaction to any chemical irritant and that he died of natural causes.

What are you some sort of Trump supporter?

No, I was just curious about your bumper sticker.

And I walked away.

And that is the trick. Not getting upset. Not defending. Just asking questions and pointing out facts and if they get confrontational just walk away.

replies.title: Re: Numerology (humour)
replies.content: In Myanmar it gets even weirder. The military government there introduced a while ago money bills with the denominations of 45 Kyat and 90 Kyat, because of local numerological superstitions (Kyat is the local currency).

replies.title: Re: Numerology (humour)
replies.content:
Silly as it seems, if it's backed up by focused will and imagination, it might accomplish something. Numerological symbols are as viable as magical foci as symbols of any other kind.

Let's look at the word WOKE for a moment. Numerologically, that's a 9 vibration -(W = 5, O = 6, K = 2, E = 5, 5 + 6 + 2 + 5 = 18, 1 + 8 = 9) and 9 is the number of achievement, so it's a powerful word -- though 9 can also be the number of pride that goeth before a fall. Change it to WOAK, which adds to 14, and you've got a 5 vibration, which is unstable, adventurous, playful, and lucky. I'd suggest instead WOK or, if you want to be deliberately silly about it -- always a good plan -- WŌK, with the macron accent over the O, or WÖK, with the umlaut. That adds to the unlucky 13, and it's also a 4 vibration, which is an energy of stability at best and very often turns into stasis and drudgery. Start consistently writing the word WŌK while concentrating on how very, very dull it all is, and watch what happens.

(Yes, this means that you'll bring the same 4-vibration into your own life. Concentrate on expressing that through being more dependable, responsible, orderly, and patient, and you can put the energy to good use. William Walker Atkinson describes this sort of direction of influences as "mental alchemy" in The Kybalion; it's a good skill to have.)

replies.title: Re: Civil War in France?
replies.content:
This episode has been extremely poorly reported in the English-language media, and it would take a long time and far too many words to go through all the background. The article (signed by a large number of retired military officers) used language which was certainly intemperate in places, but the vast majority of French people have no problem with Islam, and vice versa. That's not the issue.
The problem is violence by a small number of Islamist extremists (ie believers in political Islam, the superiority of religion over the authority of the state) which claimed another victim last month. The state seems powerless to stop it, although preventing individual attacks is acknowledged to be virtually impossible. This is linked with increasing violence and despair in the poorer suburbs of the big cities, itself a result of decades of neglect by successive governments entirely captured by the PMC, who assumed that you could basically neglect ordinary peoples' interests indefinitely. In turn, the lack of interest by the state has allowed extremist groups of all kinds to flourish. It's also linked to a constant campaign of denigration of the country's history, with which Macron himself, with his liberal, individualist, transnational outlook is associated. It also looks back to the Gilets jaunes protests of a couple of years ago - the letter objected to the use of the police against the demonstrators.
Although there have been attempts from both Left and Right, for different reasons, to turn this into a Muslim/anti-Muslim issue, that's not how ordinary people see it. There will be no civil war because for that you need two sides, and there aren't two sides. There are figures involved with the letter associated with the extreme Catholic Right, but they are a very small minority in the country. Opinion polls have shown that the majority of French people basically agree with the analysis in the letter, and around half think that if the government doesn't sort things out, the military should do it. But nobody believes a military coup is remotely possible (it would take too long to explain, but trust me). What this represents is popular frustration with a technocratic PMC government that is simply uninterested in the lives of ordinary people. The letter, like other, similar initiatives, is a rather clumsy attempt to frighten the government into becoming interested again.

I felt her presence and it firmed up what I needed to do with respect to my present career situation (basically the time has come to strike out on my own but I am risk averse). JMG, I asked you in previous posts about how to find a god and my experience in starting a prayer practice. replies.title: Re: Interesting Experience
replies.content: That's a classic way of entering into contact with a deity -- the sense of awareness and the synchronistic response to the offering are standard signs. So you've found a deity; I'd encourage you to make those offerings, keep praying, and follow the guidance you'll receive.

replies.title: Re: Interesting Experience
replies.content:
An examination of how weak English is for this sort of thing:

That distinctive feeling that comes up with a synchronicity like an owl statue, would explicitly be described as an omen in times past. Greek would subdivide it into Thaumos (wonder) if it is a positive experience, like this was; or Stigma, when such a synchronicity is clearly punishment for something you did wrong earlier.

Ancient Rome produced religious dictionaries full of technical jargon and very careful distinctions and shades of meaning. A presence that is pervasive but not localized is a Numen, if it is concentrated in a particular area in space, it becomes an Anima.

A god, if we go by etymology, is just something that we pour libations to. Athena is god, but what about local nature spirits, they receive offerings too. The Scandinavians made a distinction between Aesir (literally either lords or beings) and several other entire classes of beings that were venerated in similar rituals (elves, disir, vanir, etc.). Modern thought talks about local gods in some cultures, (notice how in the middle ages every town had its own patron saint). A god that exists in a particular locale was known to ancient Greece as a Daemon (not a demon, no negative connotations, originally), rather than a Theos. I believe Plutarch explicitly distinguished between a daemon Aphrodite in Ephesus and a goddess (thea) Aphrodite Aphrodisias venerated in the same city. Even more comically, there is an account of a Greek city getting sick of their local Daemon, and having it cast out and replaced with a new one with the help of the gods. China has a few accounts of similar things.

Offering is the usual rendering for things dedicated to a god and then respectfully put aside, like your olive oil. Other languages (and the vocabulary escapes me right now) would make distinctions between types of offering based on whether or not it requires disposal (incense is counted differently from bowls of rice and sake in Shinto for instance), and whether it is given in devotion or as a request. The old testament of the bible for instance, uses several words for sacrifice depending on whether it was a regular, scheduled thing, or if it was being done for a specific purpose.

Congratulations on your new relationship though.

comments.title: The Green Chapel
comments.content:
Here is a Musical Magic Monday offering, a single from a forthcoming album by Alasdair Roberts in collaboation with Volvur.

...about this song, ""The Green Chapel”, touches upon the ancient notion in Celtic culture of “the three noble strains” of music: geantraí, goltraí and suantraí (the strains of joy, lamentation and sleep). These are the three intertwining threads from which the fabric of a music is woven. In 'The Green Chapel' the three knot together, like wind, wave and wood, to form a syncretically co-existing wholeness "

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eCAQzcEL56c

In the video above it is performed solo on acoustic guitar.

"In January 2019, at the invitation of fiddler Hans Kjorstad, Alasdair Roberts traveled from his home in Glasgow, Scotland to Oslo, Norway, where the two men convened with five additional Scandinavian musicians at Riksscenen, Oslo’s centre for Norwegian traditional arts and music. The group was named Völvur (The Seeresses), a reference to the ancient Icelandic apocalyptic text Völuspa (The Prophecy of the Seeress).

The musicians who make up Völvur — Marthe Lea, Fredrik Rasten, Andreas Hoem Røysum, Egil Kalman, Jan Martin Gismervik and the aforementioned initiator of the project, Hans Kjorstad — are a busy and artistically inquisitive group, involved in a diverse range of projects with a wide variety of musical interests, from folk and jazz to free music, modular synthesis, microtonality and beyond. They make an ideal pairing for such voyages in the alchemical world as Alasdair pursues in his own music.

In January 2020, Völvur visited England and Scotland, to perform with Alasdair Roberts at [Cecil Sharp House] London and at [Platform], Glasgow, [the latter as part of Celtic Connections festival.] The group had new material — freshly written songs by Alasdair and several traditional Norwegian songs sung by Marthe Lea — and over a couple days at Sam and Rachel’s Studio, [Hackney,] laid down the music which now flows forth as The Old Fabled River.

On The Old Fabled River, Alasdair Roberts og Völvur meld their worlds: fiddle and vocal styles formed in the Norwegian valleys blending now with exploratory clarinet, saxophone and metallic bowed guitar drones, now fashioned into baroque folk arrangements. In one case, instrumental accompaniment is laid aside, as three voices locate a questing fullness harmonizing together."

The entire album comes out July 23rd from Drag City and I'll definitely be looking forward to it.

The Etruscan (pre-Romans who lived in the same area) religious rituals were partially preserved and kept up by the Romans. Personally,

I was raised Christian (baptist). Never felt a thing growing up. Apostatized to Buddhism, and was shocked by how easy it was to make some kind of contact.

Nasty side effect of such an upbringing is that I still think about it from time to time. The only Jesus I ever made contact with was clearly a demon. Got the holy spirit once though, it (or she, supported by language and an experience) didn't seem terribly concerned that I wasn't a Christian and explicitly pointed out I was better off and more useful elsewhere.

The Tetragrammaton exists outside of Christianity. While studying Cabala on the side, I had no difficulty getting contact with something under that name. My one Jewish friend complained that the reformed rites seem to be lacking power, but G-d picks up the slack from time to time, and they are deeply grateful for it every time. Contrast this with Catholicism, where god appears to be AWOL and the rites don't work anymore.

It may be that Christ was never a proper god. The church's formula is that he was fully god and fully man. The greatest Christian of my acquaintance thinks this is backwards; Jesus is neither god nor man nor deficient in either way, according to him. In either case, the father is alive and well.

replies.content:

comments.title: OBEs/NDEs as Sources of Metaphysical Knowledge
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

As a misguided youth, lonely and desirous of belonging, I once got really drunk and really high with a bunch of more experienced peers I was hoping to fit in with and passed out on a couch. One of these peers didn’t care for this and demanded I be taken away from the gathering on account of the eyesore that I was to everyone else at the gathering. I was able to hear all of this despite being otherwise out of order.

Another partygoer came over to me to wake me up and get me out of there and, upon tugging on my arm and yelling my name, I exited my body, floated up through the house I was in, and out into the nighttime environs which I observed from above the roof of the house. It was quite a sight! Blades of grass, various plants and trees, all were aglow with pulsating energy-light. Floating there, taking it in, I felt compelled to look above me and discovered there were several “light beings” floating there, observing me. I attempted to float up to them but was held back by something. They “laughed” in a good natured way when I attempted to do this which was observable by the light in them flickering strongly. They telepathically sent me a bunch of insights which felt like profound revelations at the time:

1. We are all profoundly loved and admired by these beings - particularly for our courage to take on bodies and exist in this highly reduced state of awareness we generally embody in which we exist unaware of the this greater reality.

2. That despite all contrary appearances, everything is perfect.

That was pretty much it. It looks like sheer drivel now, written out in words all these years later. We’ve all heard these things from NDErs and New Age woo peddlers with too much jewelry and makeup on. BUT… in the experience, at the time, I was blown away by it and utterly convinced of the truth of it. For a time, I went from being a jaded, self-centered jerk to just being in outright awe of life, the plants, the animals, the sky, the dirt, and especially people. I felt so much compassion and love and tenderness toward people and had no ability to express it without looking and sounding like a total weirdo. Unlike NDErs, my sense of this event has sort of dulled over time. There’s been plenty of times where I’ve doubted the whole thing and wondered if the cannabis and cheap beer couldn’t account for the whole thing… but I’ve had cannabis and a lot of cheap beer since then and never had anything like this happen again.

What do you think of my experience and similar experiences out there? What are these entities of light? Can they be contacted? What’s up with the light pulsating in the plants (I’ve encountered other OBErs/NDErs describing this same phenomenon, which makes me believe it’s pointing to something real). Is this direct perception of the “divinity of life” for lack of a better term accurate? How do we access that kind of awareness more regularly and intentionally?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: OBEs/NDEs as Sources of Metaphysical Knowledge
replies.content:
That is so lovely! I haven't had any experiences as intense and vivid as this, but I have had sort of "bursts" of feeling light and airy and expansive, and feeling like you have said. Also, a Daoist teacher of mine said he once met a glowing golden-light walrus spirit that was deeply awestruck by the tough and admirable choice that humans had made to incarnate, so that kind of accords with what you are saying!

-Derpherder

replies.title: Re: OBEs/NDEs as Sources of Metaphysical Knowledge
replies.content: It's a well-known kind of visionary experience -- and don't let people tell you that NDE experiences never fade over time; as usual, that varies from person to person. Since I wasn't there at the time, I can't tell you what exact type of beings you experienced -- there are lots of different kinds of nonphysical beings.  As for reaching that kind of awareness regularly and intentionally, when you can do that you will have evolved beyond the human level. Regular meditation and other spiritual practices will help you get there, but it may take you more than one life.

replies.content: Which other spiritual practices?

replies.title: Re: OBEs/NDEs as Sources of Metaphysical Knowledge
replies.content: Depends on your choice of a spiritual path. Different traditions back up meditation in different ways.

replies.title: Re: Deity contact/SOP invocation
replies.content:
I am a Shinto practitioner in that I pray and make offerings before my kamidama. I am also a western occultism practitioner, and have adapted my SOP to include the Shinto deities with whom I built prior relationship.

With adapting the SOP to include Shinto deities, my strong suggestion is to use divination before trying anything out.

With regard to offerings, I give cold water each time I pray, and then I use the previous water to make my next cup of tea. I also make a New Moon offering of food at my kamidama, which I then eat sometime afterward. I don’t know if this is traditional, but it was something that was asked of me as “it would be nice if you could do this thing but please don’t stress about it if you can’t”. In my experience, the ‘feeling’ of this is closest to being at a shrine in Japan when I am able to make this offering during the moon’s cazimi period - ideally when it has just started waxing (so, within 17” of coming off conjunct).

Sometimes I make offerings of other food or beverage items throughout the month - if someone gifts me with chocolates, it’s nice to offer them to the kami first. Also, I find sake, particularly as it is harder to get here, to be appreciated. But overall the key, in my experience, is an open, genuine heart. I would start with cold water, and then see where your relationships take you. Listen when you pray.

Finally, if you can get ofuda, then this is the best option of all for setting up a home shrine. In this case, my understanding is that you place it facing East, so that when you pray you face the West.

The ofuda itself should be kept private. In my case, as I have yet to be able to get a wooden kamidama, I keep my ofuda in a special envelope so that it is not visible / on display.

And personally, I ritually cleanse, bow twice, and clap twice before praying. Then afterward I bow once. As if I was at a shrine.

The cleansing procedure I adapted for home use is similar to this, but with my hand as the ladle and my faucet as the water source: https://www.tokyocreative.com/articles/18702-understanding-shinto-part-i-cleansing-before-entering

Best of luck.

replies.title: Re: Deity contact/SOP invocation
replies.content:
I practice Shinto but I don't call on the kami for my SOP. For what it's worth, were I to do so, I would also choose to go with Amaterasu and Okuninushi for the sky and land deities.

I second JMG's suggestion of Evans' Norito book. Also I haven't poked around there in a while, but I believe that the Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America has information about it in English somewhere.

Just to get you started: uncooked rice, water, salt, and optionally sake are the traditional core elements. Candles are also common. Use white dishes or vessels if you can. That will probably be enough to start. I would also would recommend designating a place to be your kamidana (Shinto style altar), which can be as simple as a dedicated high shelf on a bookcase or the top of a tall dresser-- preferably just above eye level. The important thing is that it be set up in a way that it can be kept clean, and that you feel is set up respectfully. (Oh, and people shouldn't be able to walk below or above it. If that's unavoidable, a symbolic kanji 雲, literally meaning "cloud", representing the open sky should be placed above the kamidana.)

Normally, there is also an ofuda (a sacred card with the kami's name written on it) on the kamidana, which the offerings are placed in front of, and serves as a symbolic and I guess material point of contact with the kami. However unless you live in Japan, which it seems you don't, ofuda may be a bit difficult to come by and so I'd recommend starting with a home setup like I've described and adding in ofuda when circumstances permit.

If you are in America, Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America sells ofuda to Amaterasu Omikami by mail. If you contact me by private message or by leaving some sort of contact information here, I'm happy to help you to connect with sources of ofuda for Ookuninushi Omikami and Konohanasakuya Hime in Japan. Alas it is not yet common for shrines to send ofuda in the mail-- it's not illegal, but shrines don't want to come across as an economic enterprise subsisting on mail orders, and generally people are encouraged to get their ofuda in person directly from a shrine.

replies.title: Re: Deity contact/SOP invocation
replies.content:
Fantastic and thorough, thanks! Do you only use one kami's ofuda for the kamidana, or rotate them out according to the season, or is it okay to have multiple?

Is there any reason why you don't call on kami for the SOP? Do you think it would be disrespectful to call on a deity for the SOP, but not give them a space on the kamidana? It certainly sounds like some ofuda are hard to come by!

Sorry if the questions are annoying, super appreciate the thoughtful and informed response!

replies.title: Re: Deity contact/SOP invocation
replies.content:
>>Do you only use one kami's ofuda for the kamidana, or rotate them out according to the season, or is it okay to have multiple?

You would put all of the ofuda on the kamidana. Don't keep them in storage. There's a certain sense that they are considered to be alive, as well-- they are meant to be replaced once a year (and disposed of in a shrine-- though as you wouldn't have one handy, respectfully burning them over prayers of thanks would probably do).

As for how many to have, it's kind of like saying, "How many close friends should one have?" The common understanding is that one not have too many ofuda. The basic form is to have an Amaterasu ofuda from Ise Jingu (this is the kind that the US Tsubaki Shrine offers), and an ofuda from the nearest shrine to where one lives. Also many people have an ofuda from their birthplace or a kami associated with one's family, and it's also common to have an ofuda of a kami related to one's profession or otherwise strongly identified with.

Of course, "Shinto wa jiyuu" ("Shinto is free") is a common expression in Shinto circles -- kind of the Shinto version of "Ask three Druids, get five answers" -- and there are no rules that you must stop at a certain number. If the kami seem to be giving you instructions to fill your kamidana up with twelve ofuda, then maybe you should! However as a rule of thumb it's probably best to stick to a number that you are capable of maintaining active spiritual communication with each.

>>Is there any reason why you don't call on kami for the SOP?

It is simply that divination suggested another set of deities was better suited for me. It may have to do with the fact that they matched the course of study I began at the same time as I began practicing the SOP. I trust that if it were important to the kami that I work with them in the SOP, they would have changed the course of that divination. Anyway, I agree with the poster above that some form of reality checking is important, in some combination of prayer and divination, to make sure that the kami you call on are happy to join you in this fashion in your SOP.

>>Do you think it would be disrespectful to call on a deity for the SOP, but not give them a space on the kamidana?

I think it would be fine to call on a kami without giving them space on your kamidana. Just be respectful when you call on them. If they want you to have an ofuda associated with them on their kamidana as well, they will probably let you know in some form!

One thing to bear in mind with ofuda is that they are always location-specific: an emblem of that kami, as they express via one specific shrine. The same kami often express in different forms via different shrines! Of the three you named, Amaterasu and Ookuninushi both have a main shrine which is the one everybody knows is "top" (Amaterasu = Ise Jingu, Ookuninushi = Izumo Taisha). Konohanasakuya Hime as you surely know is associated with Mount Fuji but choosing an Ofuda source may take some research.

>>It certainly sounds like some ofuda are hard to come by!

Perhaps, but at least for the most commonly worshipped kami-- of which the three that you named definitely are-- it's not too hard, as long as you live in Japan! :-) Anyway, as I said, if you are interested in going that far, I'm happy to help you with any ofuda difficulties.

replies.title: Re: Deity contact/SOP invocation
replies.content:
You've possibly discovered them already, but beyond the website of the Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America, I will vouch for the Green Shinto blog and the Onmark Productions visual dictionary as two websites with a great deal of interesting information for somebody who is starting out on their Shinto journey.

One thing I should have made clear when describing convenient possible ways to set up a kamidana, is that by far the most common kamidana setup is a single shelf placed on a wall for that purpose.

Also, previous commenter was correct that ofuda don’t usually get kept out in the open, but instead are hidden behind a cover. I hope this isn’t all starting to sound too complicated— it’s really not.

comments.title: Polytheist Rose Cross
comments.content: I came across your Polytheistc Ceremonial Magic section of Llewellyn's Ceremonial Magic book and was wondering if you ever put together a polytheistic Rose Cross equivalent? I'm working with Egyptian deities at the moment, primarily Bast and Sekhmet if that makes a difference.

replies.title: Re: Polytheist Rose Cross
replies.content: Each polytheist tradition needs its own such ritual, since the symbolism and energies are different in each case -- for example, in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn there's a Rose+Cross equivalent that works with Druid deities and symbolism, and it's just as idiosyncratic as the R+C ritual itself.

replies.title: Re: Polytheist Rose Cross
replies.content:
In light of the thought that the energies themselves could be different I'm realizing I was thinking of the Rose+Cross as its own self-contained ritual like I think of the LRP, but now I'm suddenly looking at it like I would a wand consecration ritual: a specific case of a wide open generic consecration. I'm seeing the pattern of symbol in the 6 directions followed by a transformation of the symbol and an invocation could be applied to almost anything depending on the symbolism and invocation.

Do you have any guidance with regards to this ritual's niche in daily or weekly practice one should consider when rolling their own? Was there any specific niche you filled in the CGD by translating the R+C beyond just giving a tool for blessing and protection? You blew my mind all over the walls, and I think I need some help reeling it in. I fear it's rosy crosses all the way down... the horror!

replies.title: Re: Polytheist Rose Cross
replies.content:
Excellent! Yes, that's just it -- it isn't a self-contained ritual, because it inevitably relates to the highest vision of redemptive divine power within the tradition on which it's based. So you have to take the theology of that tradition and use that to come up with a coherent analysis of the process of inner transformation, then express that in symbolic terms.

R+C-style rituals are not necessarily well suited to daily practice unless you're in very serious spiritual trouble. They're rituals of aspiration toward the highest; once a week is good for most people.
